text.title: Chaos Engineering Practice for Infra Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every Chaos Engineering Practice for Infra application must enforce. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Define the steady-state hypothesis in metric form (e.g., 'p95 latency <200ms, error rate <0.5%') BEFORE the experiment. No hypothesis = no experiment.
rule.rationale: Chaos without baseline is sabotage; steady-state is the scientific anchor.

rule.statement: Every experiment has explicit blast radius: which services, % of traffic, geographies, duration. Cap is enforced by tooling (flag, namespace, pct).
rule.rationale: Uncapped chaos becomes an incident; tooling-enforced caps protect bystanders.

rule.statement: First 6+ game-days run in business hours with full team on-call presence. After 6 successful daytime runs, may escalate to off-hours.
rule.rationale: Off-hours chaos amplifies blast radius (oncall solo) + impairs learning; daytime maximizes safe learning.

rule.statement: Each experiment targets a service with an existing runbook. If runbook is missing, write it first; chaos exposes runbook gaps but cannot replace runbooks.
rule.rationale: Chaos without runbook = experimental damage with no recovery path.

rule.statement: Every game-day produces ≥1 concrete improvement action (code, config, runbook, observability) with owner + deadline. No action = ritual without progress.
rule.rationale: Without committed action, experiments degrade to demos; one action per game-day compounds.
